EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including at least one tree blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In only a few hours' time between the late evening of Sunday the 28th and the early morning of Monday the 29th, a nearly stationary cluster of thunderstorms drenched much of southern Adams, northern Webster and adjacent small portions of Franklin and Nuckolls counties with 3-7 inches of heavy rain, at least temporarily halting a worsening drought (more on this below). Extensive flooding ensued along the Little Blue River and its tributaries (including Ash Creek) and numerous rural county roads/bridges were flooded out/damaged, some of which remained barricaded for several days. The overall-worst flooding concentrated within south central Adams County, including the Crystal Lake Recreation Area and campground located just north of Ayr along the Little Blue River. There, extensive damage occurred to electrical boxes, buildings and picnic tables, and the park remained closed to the public for over two weeks before reopening in mid-September. Along the Little Blue itself, flood waters quickly worked downstream from Ayr, reaching the Pauline area on the morning of the 29th and the automated river gauge near Deweese by early evening. At this gauge site, the river crested around 11 feet (one foot above minor flood stage) during the overnight hours before falling below flood stage on the morning of the 30th. Minor flooding likely continued along the river through much of northeastern Nuckolls County, but no flooding was reported farther downstream into Thayer County. This event was all about flooding, as thunderstorms otherwise remained sub-severe and yielded no reports of large hail or damaging winds. ||This was likely the most notable flooding event to strike the Adams/Webster County line area in several decades, especially considering that the majority of accumulation occurred in only a four-hour period between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. CDT. Some of the most impressive rain totals featured: 7.27 two miles northeast of Blue Hill (NeRAIN observer), 6.62 four miles southwest of Blue Hill (NWS observer), 5.60 four miles south-southeast of Roseland (NeRAIN), 5.50 in Blue Hill (personal weather station) and 3.81 five miles south-southeast of Deweese (NeRAIN). In an ironic twist, leading up to this event the southern Adams/northern Webster locale had been one of the driest places in the entire state of Nebraska for the summer, having recently been placed in a Severe Drought (D2) category by the U.S. Drought Monitor. As a striking example of this sudden transition from drought to flood, the NWS cooperative observer near Blue Hill tallied merely 2.77 of rain from Jun 1-Aug 27 (roughly 25% of normal) and then received more than twice this amount (6.62) on this single night! ||Taking a closer look at event timing and evolution, things got underway between 9:30-10:30 p.m. CDT on the 28th as thunderstorms erupted along a northwest-southeast axis extending from western Adams County down into Republic/Washington counties in Kansas. Over the next several hours, the northwestern edge of this initial activity evolved into a quasi-stationary storm cluster that essentially anchored itself over the southern Adams/northern Webster area. By 2 a.m. CDT on the 29th, convection finally began to diminish in intensity/rainfall rates, leaving only lingering lighter showers that dissipated completely by 5 a.m. CDT. This was a rather sneaky mesoscale flood event, as although low chances for thunderstorms were in the forecast, computer models (even in the very short term) were not spotlighting the potential for significant flooding. Not surprisingly, forcing aloft was rather nebulous, not uncommon by late-August standards. In the mid-upper levels, the Central Plains resided under fairly weak south-southwest flow, directed between broad ridging over the southeastern CONUS and a slow-moving trough to the west anchored by a modest closed low over New Mexico. Parameters were not conducive to severe storms given deep-layer wind shear averaging well under 30 knots and weak mid-level lapse rates. However, favorable factors for slow-moving, heavy rainers included: the presence of subtle mid-level waves, precipitable water values averaging over 1.5 and slightly-enhanced flow between 850-700 millibars during the night, resulting in increased deep moisture convergence. At the surface, there were no notable features to speak of, although dewpoints averaging in the mid-60s F were indicative of the moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm-total rainfall of 7.27 inches...all of which fell between 9:30 p.m. and 5 a.m. CDT.

As a striking example of this sudden transition from drought to flood, the NWS cooperative observer near Blue Hill tallied merely 2.77 of rain from Jun 1-Aug 27 (roughly 25% of normal) and then received more than twice this amount (6.62) on this single night! ||Taking a closer look at event timing and evolution, things got underway between 9:30-10:30 p.m. CDT on the 28th as thunderstorms erupted along a northwest-southeast axis extending from western Adams County down into Republic/Washington counties in Kansas. Over the next several hours, the northwestern edge of this initial activity evolved into a quasi-stationary storm cluster that essentially anchored itself over the southern Adams/northern Webster area. By 2 a.m. CDT on the 29th, convection finally began to diminish in intensity/rainfall rates, leaving only lingering lighter showers that dissipated completely by 5 a.m. CDT. This was a rather sneaky mesoscale flood event, as although low chances for thunderstorms were in the forecast, computer models (even in the very short term) were not spotlighting the potential for significant flooding. Not surprisingly, forcing aloft was rather nebulous, not uncommon by late-August standards. In the mid-upper levels, the Central Plains resided under fairly weak south-southwest flow, directed between broad ridging over the southeastern CONUS and a slow-moving trough to the west anchored by a modest closed low over New Mexico. Parameters were not conducive to severe storms given deep-layer wind shear averaging well under 30 knots and weak mid-level lapse rates. However, favorable factors for slow-moving, heavy rainers included: the presence of subtle mid-level waves, precipitable water values averaging over 1.5 and slightly-enhanced flow between 850-700 millibars during the night, resulting in increased deep moisture convergence. At the surface, there were no notable features to speak of, although dewpoints averaging in the mid-60s F were indicative of the moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 5.50 inches...all of which fell between 9:30 p.m. and 5 a.m. CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds at Homer in Dakota County in extreme northeast Nebraska around noon on August 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Within South Central Nebraska, verified severe weather (in the form of hail up to golf ball size) was solely confined to Furnas County on this late Monday afternoon and evening, but it was a noteworthy situation as nearly the exact same localized area remained under a perpetual Severe Thunderstorm Warning (actually six separate/consecutive warnings) for more than 3.5 hours from 5:49 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. CDT! This resulted from a quasi-stationary, multicell storm cluster remaining anchored over the Furnas/Red Willow County line...just as one storm core would weaken or start to drift slightly to the east, another would pop up and take its place. At long last, possibly in response to a loss of daytime heating, this storm cluster started weakening and drifting east after 9 p.m. CDT, finally dissipating for good in southeastern Furnas County around 11 p.m. CDT. The vast majority of this activity concentrated in the far northwestern and west central part of the county, from around Cambridge southward through rural areas northwest of Wilsonville. In this area, there were several reports of penny to half dollar size hail in and very near Cambridge, with golf ball size stones falling several miles south of town. Not surprisingly given the persistent storm generation, radar rainfall estimates in far west-central Furnas County spiked to at least 3-4 inches. However, there were no ground-truth reports of extremely heavy rain or flash flooding from this bullseye, and official reports from Cambridge itself yielded less than 2 with only minor road flooding. ||In the wake of the Furnas County activity, the late evening and overnight hours featured a completely separate and more widespread area of occasionally-strong storms primarily within the far northern and eastern reaches of South Central Nebraska. This nocturnal activity, which really got going between 11 p.m. and 1 a.m. CDT, actually persisted all night and into the next morning in some weakened form before finally exiting the Thayer County area around 10 a.m. CDT on the 2nd. Although radar indicated some potential for hail with a few of these overnight storms, heavy rain was the main feature as a good chunk of counties such as Greeley/Nance/Polk measured at least 1-2 and locally higher (including 2.90 south of Spalding). However, like the earlier Furnas County storms, there were no reports of significant flooding. ||In the mid-upper levels, this was a pretty typical and weakly-forced mid-summer event as South Central Nebraska resided under fairly weak quasi-zonal flow between ridging over the southern CONUS and a potent shortwave trough well to the north over south central Canada. The Furnas County severe storms likely initiated from the combination of a subtle mid-level wave and intense heating/weak convergence along a surface trough aligned from western KS into central NE. With temperatures in the Furnas County area reaching the mid-upper 90s F and dewpoints in the low-mid 60s, convection was able to overcome warm mid level temperatures and become severe in an environment characterized by mixed-layer CAPE of at least 2000 J/kg but only around 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear. Later in the night, the uptick in nocturnal convection occurred in classic fashion near the nose of a 30-40 knot low-level jet evident around 850 millibars.

Two tornadoes occurred along with hail up to the size of golf balls on this Monday afternoon. A few very isolated general thunderstorms developed across south central Nebraska beginning around 1:30 p.m. CST. However, storms did not become severe until after 3 p.m. CST. Severe hail up to the size of golf balls occurred with some storms, which was impressive given the weak shear and pulse-type nature of the storms. A non-supercell tornado occurred near Odessa, in Buffalo County. Somehow, a lone supercell managed to develop over Clay County, and it was responsible for producing a tornado and hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter. Both of these tornadoes were rated EF-0. By 7 p.m. CST, all of the thunderstorm activity had dissipated or moved out of south central Nebraska.||Surface low pressure was quasi-stationary over western Kansas. The warm front extending east from the low lifted into central Nebraska and became stationary. Just prior to most of the thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the lower to middle 70s with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. With mid-level lapse rates near 8 degrees C/km, this resulted in MLCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 15-20 knots, barely supportive of multicell storm mode. In the upper levels, a longwave trough was over the western U.S. with a lead low exiting Nebraska into South Dakota, and a new more potent low dropping into the backside of the trough over the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado touched down near Odessa. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced mostly hail along with a little wind damage, and excessive rainfall on this Tuesday afternoon and evening. The first thunderstorms began forming just after 3 pm and were part of a broken line of isolated storms that extended from O'neill south to near Kearney, and then southwest into Kansas. A dominant storm developed along the Hall-Howard County line by 4 p.m.. This storm became severe as it continued east, affecting adjoining portions of Hamilton and Merrick Counties through 5:30 pm CST. Around 4:50 pm CST, this storm produced a gustnado near Chapman, along with hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter, and estimated winds around 70 mph. After 5:30 pm, other storms developed in close-proximity, displaying multicell characteristics. An east-west oriented line segment of storms formed across Hamilton and York Counties. This line remained intact until 8 p.m. CST. Storms within this line trained, producing severe hail up to the size of golf balls and flash flooding from several inches of rain. Outflow winds overturned an irrigation pivot and produced blowing dust that reduced the visibility to near zero near Bradshaw. Several reports of gustnadoes were received along the leading edge of the outflow. ||Meanwhile, one storm crossed into Furnas County from Kansas beginning around 4 p.m. CST. Several other thunderstorms developed around it, forming a small mostly sub-severe cluster. The initial storm briefly became severe, producing estimated winds of 85 mph in northern Furnas County. This cluster moved northeast and eventually formed a short line segment that was oriented from northwest to southeast. This line produced winds of 50 to 55 mph in a couple locations in Adams and Clay Counties. It then crossed Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties from 7 to 9 p.m. CST, adding to the already impressive rainfall amounts. Much of northeastern Hamilton County received 4 to 6 inches of rain in less than 6 hours. The highest total was 6.77 inches at Marquette. These high rainfall amounts were surrounded by a larger area of 2 to 3 inches which extended across Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties. This rain resulted in extensive flooding over northeastern Hamilton County and along the Big Blue River in Polk County.||Occluding low pressure was along the Montana-North Dakota border on this day. The triple point was over South Dakota, with a quasi-stationary warm front extending east through the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic States. The cold front was advancing eastward across Nebraska, and was moving through south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours. In the upper levels, a broad trough covered much of the Unites States, anchored by an intensifying upper-low over Montana. A closed high was over Ontario, Canada. Temperatures in the pre-storm environment climbed to around 80 degrees, with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor resulting in MLCAPE of not more than 1500 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 30 to 35 knots.

While general thunderstorm activity affected much of South Central Nebraska at some point between the late afternoon of Thursday the 11th and the early morning of Friday the 12th, the only severe-criteria wind/hail reports came from the far southeastern and western reaches of the 24-county area. There was also a lone report from a storm chaser of a weak, brief EF0 rope tornado in extreme southern Sherman County around sunset. Generally speaking, this event consisted of two distinct rounds of strong to severe storms over roughly an eight-hour period, with a decent break in between. During round 1, which focused during the early evening from 6-8 p.m. CDT, a quick-moving convective cluster charged east-northeast through mainly Nuckolls and Thayer counties, resulting in several reports of estimated 50-70 MPH wind gusts, highlighted by estimated 70 MPH gusts west of Deshler and 65 MPH near Belvidere. As this initial round was in the process of departing the local area into southeast Nebraska between 8-9 p.m. CDT, attention shifted off to the northwest as an isolated cell with occasional supercell characteristics drifted into southern Sherman County from the west. Although radar data suggested this storm was in the process of weakening, it nonetheless produced the aforementioned brief tornado southwest of Rockville before fading away. From 10 p.m. to 1 a.m. CDT, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska found itself storm-free as all active weather focused off to the east and also northwest. Eventually though, round 2 of strong to severe storms targeted primarily Dawson County between 1-2 a.m. CDT as a broken line of convection over western Nebraska briefly morphed into a small-scale bow echo. This activity yielded reports of quarter size hail in Gothenburg, estimated 60 MPH winds with tree limbs down in Cozad and a measured 56 MPH gust at the Lexington airport. However, this storm complex soon weakened below severe thresholds as it tracked deeper into the local area, and although isolated to scattered storms persisted across various parts of South Central Nebraska until around 5 a.m. CDT on the 29th there were no additional severe weather reports. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of the area averaged well-under 1 inch, with some pockets of 1-2 concentrated mainly within Nuckolls/Thayer counties from round 1.||Given that this event consisted of different rounds of storms separated in both location and time, it should come as no surprise that the upper air and surface situation was somewhat complex. Starting with the mid-upper levels, forcing was respectable for mid-August as South Central Nebraska was positioned downstream from a broad, slow-moving trough edging from the Northern Rockies toward the Dakotas. Embedded within this mean trough were several smaller-scale shortwaves, each helping to drive the separate rounds of convection. At the surface, a very slow-moving/weak cold front gradually worked its way southeastward through South Central Nebraska during the evening/overnight. Digging a bit deeper, round 1 during the early evening was largely driven by weak convergence and considerable instability focused along a pre-frontal surface trough stretched from southwest KS into southeast NE. Despite fairly weak deep-layer wind shear only around 30 knots, these storms quickly became severe in an environment featuring at least 2000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and surface dewpoints well into the low-mid 70s F. The second round of storms that arrived later in the night initially developed during the evening well to the west over the High Plains and was tied more closely to the primary mid level vort max approaching from WY and also the actual surface cold front. Even at this late hour, round 2 still had up to around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE to work with.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were estimated by a trained spotter. Tree limbs up to four inches in diameter snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two tornadoes occurred along with hail up to the size of golf balls on this Monday afternoon. A few very isolated general thunderstorms developed across south central Nebraska beginning around 1:30 p.m. CST. However, storms did not become severe until after 3 p.m. CST. Severe hail up to the size of golf balls occurred with some storms, which was impressive given the weak shear and pulse-type nature of the storms. A non-supercell tornado occurred near Odessa, in Buffalo County. Somehow, a lone supercell managed to develop over Clay County, and it was responsible for producing a tornado and hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter. Both of these tornadoes were rated EF-0. By 7 p.m. CST, all of the thunderstorm activity had dissipated or moved out of south central Nebraska.||Surface low pressure was quasi-stationary over western Kansas. The warm front extending east from the low lifted into central Nebraska and became stationary. Just prior to most of the thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the lower to middle 70s with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. With mid-level lapse rates near 8 degrees C/km, this resulted in MLCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 15-20 knots, barely supportive of multicell storm mode. In the upper levels, a longwave trough was over the western U.S. with a lead low exiting Nebraska into South Dakota, and a new more potent low dropping into the backside of the trough over the Northern Rockies.
A few isolated thunderstorms developed along a southwest to northeast oriented wind shift line across southwest and north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind speed measured at North Platte Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple isolated severe thunderstorms developed and moved across north central Nebraska during the overnight hours of August 15, 2016.

As an upper level storm system moved into the northern Plains, a warm front retreated through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the afternoon. Unseasonably warm and humid air lifted into the region creating marginal instability by mid afternoon, but with the warm front in the area environmental shear was strong. Thunderstorms developed across northeast and east-central Nebraska by mid afternoon. Initially isolated supercells were the dominate mode with large hail the main threat. A small short-lived tornado did develop from one of the supercells in Washington County. Thunderstorms then moved into western Iowa as they continued to produce large hail. By mid evening the thunderstorms developed a common outflow in a west to east orientation and allowed for training, especially over northeast Nebraska. This continued regeneration of thunderstorms over the same area led to significant river and flash flooding over parts of the through the night.

Thunderstorms produced mostly hail along with a little wind damage, and excessive rainfall on this Tuesday afternoon and evening. The first thunderstorms began forming just after 3 pm and were part of a broken line of isolated storms that extended from O'neill south to near Kearney, and then southwest into Kansas. A dominant storm developed along the Hall-Howard County line by 4 p.m.. This storm became severe as it continued east, affecting adjoining portions of Hamilton and Merrick Counties through 5:30 pm CST. Around 4:50 pm CST, this storm produced a gustnado near Chapman, along with hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter, and estimated winds around 70 mph. After 5:30 pm, other storms developed in close-proximity, displaying multicell characteristics. An east-west oriented line segment of storms formed across Hamilton and York Counties. This line remained intact until 8 p.m. CST. Storms within this line trained, producing severe hail up to the size of golf balls and flash flooding from several inches of rain. Outflow winds overturned an irrigation pivot and produced blowing dust that reduced the visibility to near zero near Bradshaw. Several reports of gustnadoes were received along the leading edge of the outflow. ||Meanwhile, one storm crossed into Furnas County from Kansas beginning around 4 p.m. CST. Several other thunderstorms developed around it, forming a small mostly sub-severe cluster. The initial storm briefly became severe, producing estimated winds of 85 mph in northern Furnas County. This cluster moved northeast and eventually formed a short line segment that was oriented from northwest to southeast. This line produced winds of 50 to 55 mph in a couple locations in Adams and Clay Counties. It then crossed Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties from 7 to 9 p.m. CST, adding to the already impressive rainfall amounts. Much of northeastern Hamilton County received 4 to 6 inches of rain in less than 6 hours. The highest total was 6.77 inches at Marquette. These high rainfall amounts were surrounded by a larger area of 2 to 3 inches which extended across Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties. This rain resulted in extensive flooding over northeastern Hamilton County and along the Big Blue River in Polk County.||Occluding low pressure was along the Montana-North Dakota border on this day. The triple point was over South Dakota, with a quasi-stationary warm front extending east through the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic States. The cold front was advancing eastward across Nebraska, and was moving through south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours. In the upper levels, a broad trough covered much of the Unites States, anchored by an intensifying upper-low over Montana. A closed high was over Ontario, Canada. Temperatures in the pre-storm environment climbed to around 80 degrees, with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor resulting in MLCAPE of not more than 1500 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 30 to 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was accompanied by 60 MPH wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms produced severe hail on this Monday late in the afternoon and into the early evening. Between noon and 1 p.m. CST, scattered showers developed over Webster and Adams Counties. There were a few embedded rumbles of thunder. These showers expanded and lifted northeast into Hall, Hamilton, and Merrick Counties. A brief non-supercell tornado touched down near Juniata, in Adams County, around 1:40 p.m. CST. This tornado occurred in the absence of any lightning and rain, although there was a shower off to the east. By 4 p.m. CST, a couple of the showers strengthened into thunderstorms. Scattered thunderstorms actually started increasing in coverage after 6 p.m. CST as an approaching shortwave trough began influencing the region. Several storms produced at least quarter size hail over Clay, Hall, Hamilton, and Webster Counties. Larger hail did occur, with tennis ball size being the largest, reported near Giltner, in southwestern Hamilton County. Car windows were broken. Between 6:30 p.m. and 8:30 p.m. CST, storms formed into a southwest-northeast oriented line across Hall and Hamilton Counties, resulting in cell-training. This resulted in rainfall totals exceeding 2 inches over Grand Island, including 2.72 inches on the northeast side of the city. Some urban flash flooding occurred, with roads being closed due to flooded underpasses. Manhole covers were also removed in some locations. The thunderstorm activity had moved east and south of south central Nebraska by 11 p.m. CST.||A north-northeast to south-southwest oriented quasi-stationary front extended from the eastern Dakota's through western Kansas into New Mexico. A lee-side low was near Colorado Springs. In the upper levels broad southwest flow was over much of the United States, with a ridge over the Great Lakes, and an elongated trough from Hudson Bay to central California. A weak shortwave trough was passing over the Central Plains. When the first storms initiated, late in the afternoon, temperatures were in the middle 70s, with dewpoints in the lower 60s. MLCAPE was between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 40 to 45 knots. This environment was more than supportive of organized, persistent storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls on this Thursday. Storms began forming early, with the first storms forming just after 12 p.m. CST over Webster, Nuckolls, and Thayer Counties. These storms rapidly intensified, merged into a solid line, and lifted north into Adams, Clay, and Fillmore Counties. These storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls in a few locations over Adams and Clay Counties. This line of storms stalled and gradually weakened from west to east. However, before the storms weakened over Fillmore county, multiple storms moved east within the line and resulted in 2.57 inches of rain falling between 1 and 3 p.m. CST in Geneva. A CoCoRaHS station reported a 24-hour total of 3.55 inches just west of Geneva. Before the tail end of this line exited Fillmore County, a couple small storms developed just to the south over Thayer County and they produced 1 inch hail in Gilead. While all this was in progress, another line of storms had formed over north central Kansas and this line lifted north as well. Just as the initial line of storms exited south central Nebraska and moved east into southeast Nebraska, this line of storms crossed the border into Franklin, Webster, Nuckolls, and Thayer Counties around 3:45 p.m. CST. The storms then started weakening as they lifted further north. However, nickel to quarter size hail fell in a few spots and a severe wind gust of 62 mph was measured near Glenvil. No other storms were severe after 5 p.m. CST.||The depiction of surface fronts was complex. Occluding low pressure was over southern Manitoba. Early in the day, a slow-moving cold front extending south from Minnesota and Iowa into Nebraska. This front appeared to weaken during the afternoon. Meanwhile, a quasi-stationary front extended from New Mexico across Kansas into Missouri. This front lifted north through the day as a warm front, but was effectively forced back to the south as thunderstorm outflows cooled temperatures over Nebraska. In the upper levels, a longwave trough was over the western United States, with broad southwest flow from Texas to New England. A subtropical high was over the western Atlantic. During the day, a shortwave trough ejected out of the Desert Southwest into the Southern Plains, aiding in thunderstorm development. Just prior to thunderstorm initiation, temperatures were generally in the upper 70s and lower 80s, with dewpoints varying from the upper 50s to lower 70s. This surface environment was overlaid with very steep mid-level lapse rates around 9 degrees C/km, resulting in MLCAPE between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Strong mid-level winds, combined with directional shear in the low-levels, produced effective deep layer shear between 40 and 50 knots.

Several storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls on this Thursday. Storms began forming early, with the first storms forming just after 12 p.m. CST over Webster, Nuckolls, and Thayer Counties. These storms rapidly intensified, merged into a solid line, and lifted north into Adams, Clay, and Fillmore Counties. These storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls in a few locations over Adams and Clay Counties. This line of storms stalled and gradually weakened from west to east. However, before the storms weakened over Fillmore county, multiple storms moved east within the line and resulted in 2.57 inches of rain falling between 1 and 3 p.m. CST in Geneva. A CoCoRaHS station reported a 24-hour total of 3.55 inches just west of Geneva. Before the tail end of this line exited Fillmore County, a couple small storms developed just to the south over Thayer County and they produced 1 inch hail in Gilead. While all this was in progress, another line of storms had formed over north central Kansas and this line lifted north as well. Just as the initial line of storms exited south central Nebraska and moved east into southeast Nebraska, this line of storms crossed the border into Franklin, Webster, Nuckolls, and Thayer Counties around 3:45 p.m. CST. The storms then started weakening as they lifted further north. However, nickel to quarter size hail fell in a few spots and a severe wind gust of 62 mph was measured near Glenvil. No other storms were severe after 5 p.m. CST.||The depiction of surface fronts was complex. Occluding low pressure was over southern Manitoba. Early in the day, a slow-moving cold front extending south from Minnesota and Iowa into Nebraska. This front appeared to weaken during the afternoon. Meanwhile, a quasi-stationary front extended from New Mexico across Kansas into Missouri. This front lifted north through the day as a warm front, but was effectively forced back to the south as thunderstorm outflows cooled temperatures over Nebraska. In the upper levels, a longwave trough was over the western United States, with broad southwest flow from Texas to New England. A subtropical high was over the western Atlantic. During the day, a shortwave trough ejected out of the Desert Southwest into the Southern Plains, aiding in thunderstorm development. Just prior to thunderstorm initiation, temperatures were generally in the upper 70s and lower 80s, with dewpoints varying from the upper 50s to lower 70s. This surface environment was overlaid with very steep mid-level lapse rates around 9 degrees C/km, resulting in MLCAPE between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Strong mid-level winds, combined with directional shear in the low-levels, produced effective deep layer shear between 40 and 50 knots.
A thunderstorm produced large hail over Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to quarter size hail was reported at Sidney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced mostly hail along with a little wind damage, and excessive rainfall on this Tuesday afternoon and evening. The first thunderstorms began forming just after 3 pm and were part of a broken line of isolated storms that extended from O'neill south to near Kearney, and then southwest into Kansas. A dominant storm developed along the Hall-Howard County line by 4 p.m.. This storm became severe as it continued east, affecting adjoining portions of Hamilton and Merrick Counties through 5:30 pm CST. Around 4:50 pm CST, this storm produced a gustnado near Chapman, along with hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter, and estimated winds around 70 mph. After 5:30 pm, other storms developed in close-proximity, displaying multicell characteristics. An east-west oriented line segment of storms formed across Hamilton and York Counties. This line remained intact until 8 p.m. CST. Storms within this line trained, producing severe hail up to the size of golf balls and flash flooding from several inches of rain. Outflow winds overturned an irrigation pivot and produced blowing dust that reduced the visibility to near zero near Bradshaw. Several reports of gustnadoes were received along the leading edge of the outflow. ||Meanwhile, one storm crossed into Furnas County from Kansas beginning around 4 p.m. CST. Several other thunderstorms developed around it, forming a small mostly sub-severe cluster. The initial storm briefly became severe, producing estimated winds of 85 mph in northern Furnas County. This cluster moved northeast and eventually formed a short line segment that was oriented from northwest to southeast. This line produced winds of 50 to 55 mph in a couple locations in Adams and Clay Counties. It then crossed Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties from 7 to 9 p.m. CST, adding to the already impressive rainfall amounts. Much of northeastern Hamilton County received 4 to 6 inches of rain in less than 6 hours. The highest total was 6.77 inches at Marquette. These high rainfall amounts were surrounded by a larger area of 2 to 3 inches which extended across Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties. This rain resulted in extensive flooding over northeastern Hamilton County and along the Big Blue River in Polk County.||Occluding low pressure was along the Montana-North Dakota border on this day. The triple point was over South Dakota, with a quasi-stationary warm front extending east through the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic States. The cold front was advancing eastward across Nebraska, and was moving through south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours. In the upper levels, a broad trough covered much of the Unites States, anchored by an intensifying upper-low over Montana. A closed high was over Ontario, Canada. Temperatures in the pre-storm environment climbed to around 80 degrees, with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor resulting in MLCAPE of not more than 1500 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 30 to 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was accompanied by 60 MPH winds.

A complex of thunderstorms developed over central Nebraska and shifted east into eastern Nebraska during the early morning hours. Although the majority of these were non-severe, a strong thunderstorm did produce wind damage in Saunders County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some tree damage around town was noted from the observer from the wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly worked south into northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa during the afternoon of August 11th as a weak storm system crossed the northern Plains. Although wind shear was weak, strong instability developed across the area as temperatures soared into the 90s with dew points in the 70s. Thunderstorms developed along the cold front in northeast Nebraska by late afternoon and moved southeast across west central Iowa and eastern Nebraska. Other thunderstorms also developed in south central Nebraska and north central Kansas and moved into southeast Nebraska. Isolated severe weather was observed with both lines of thunderstorms, along with heavy rainfall totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in the city of Fairbury that landed on a transformer and resulted in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in two areas on the evening August 16th. The first area was over northeast Nebraska along a weak stalled boundary, and the second area was along an outflow boundary over southeast Nebraska. The combination of favorable instability and shear in both of these areas allowed for marginal supercell structures and severe weather, mainly in the form of large hail. The thunderstorms dissipated by late evening with the loss of surface heating.

Within South Central Nebraska, verified severe weather (in the form of hail up to golf ball size) was solely confined to Furnas County on this late Monday afternoon and evening, but it was a noteworthy situation as nearly the exact same localized area remained under a perpetual Severe Thunderstorm Warning (actually six separate/consecutive warnings) for more than 3.5 hours from 5:49 p.m. to 9:30 p.m. CDT! This resulted from a quasi-stationary, multicell storm cluster remaining anchored over the Furnas/Red Willow County line...just as one storm core would weaken or start to drift slightly to the east, another would pop up and take its place. At long last, possibly in response to a loss of daytime heating, this storm cluster started weakening and drifting east after 9 p.m. CDT, finally dissipating for good in southeastern Furnas County around 11 p.m. CDT. The vast majority of this activity concentrated in the far northwestern and west central part of the county, from around Cambridge southward through rural areas northwest of Wilsonville. In this area, there were several reports of penny to half dollar size hail in and very near Cambridge, with golf ball size stones falling several miles south of town. Not surprisingly given the persistent storm generation, radar rainfall estimates in far west-central Furnas County spiked to at least 3-4 inches. However, there were no ground-truth reports of extremely heavy rain or flash flooding from this bullseye, and official reports from Cambridge itself yielded less than 2 with only minor road flooding. ||In the wake of the Furnas County activity, the late evening and overnight hours featured a completely separate and more widespread area of occasionally-strong storms primarily within the far northern and eastern reaches of South Central Nebraska. This nocturnal activity, which really got going between 11 p.m. and 1 a.m. CDT, actually persisted all night and into the next morning in some weakened form before finally exiting the Thayer County area around 10 a.m. CDT on the 2nd. Although radar indicated some potential for hail with a few of these overnight storms, heavy rain was the main feature as a good chunk of counties such as Greeley/Nance/Polk measured at least 1-2 and locally higher (including 2.90 south of Spalding). However, like the earlier Furnas County storms, there were no reports of significant flooding. ||In the mid-upper levels, this was a pretty typical and weakly-forced mid-summer event as South Central Nebraska resided under fairly weak quasi-zonal flow between ridging over the southern CONUS and a potent shortwave trough well to the north over south central Canada. The Furnas County severe storms likely initiated from the combination of a subtle mid-level wave and intense heating/weak convergence along a surface trough aligned from western KS into central NE. With temperatures in the Furnas County area reaching the mid-upper 90s F and dewpoints in the low-mid 60s, convection was able to overcome warm mid level temperatures and become severe in an environment characterized by mixed-layer CAPE of at least 2000 J/kg but only around 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear. Later in the night, the uptick in nocturnal convection occurred in classic fashion near the nose of a 30-40 knot low-level jet evident around 850 millibars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell along with a few golf ball size stones mixed in. Roads were also covered in hail on the south side of Cambridge as heavy rain occurred. Some roads in town were having flooding issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While general thunderstorm activity affected much of South Central Nebraska at some point between the late afternoon of Thursday the 11th and the early morning of Friday the 12th, the only severe-criteria wind/hail reports came from the far southeastern and western reaches of the 24-county area. There was also a lone report from a storm chaser of a weak, brief EF0 rope tornado in extreme southern Sherman County around sunset. Generally speaking, this event consisted of two distinct rounds of strong to severe storms over roughly an eight-hour period, with a decent break in between. During round 1, which focused during the early evening from 6-8 p.m. CDT, a quick-moving convective cluster charged east-northeast through mainly Nuckolls and Thayer counties, resulting in several reports of estimated 50-70 MPH wind gusts, highlighted by estimated 70 MPH gusts west of Deshler and 65 MPH near Belvidere. As this initial round was in the process of departing the local area into southeast Nebraska between 8-9 p.m. CDT, attention shifted off to the northwest as an isolated cell with occasional supercell characteristics drifted into southern Sherman County from the west. Although radar data suggested this storm was in the process of weakening, it nonetheless produced the aforementioned brief tornado southwest of Rockville before fading away. From 10 p.m. to 1 a.m. CDT, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska found itself storm-free as all active weather focused off to the east and also northwest. Eventually though, round 2 of strong to severe storms targeted primarily Dawson County between 1-2 a.m. CDT as a broken line of convection over western Nebraska briefly morphed into a small-scale bow echo. This activity yielded reports of quarter size hail in Gothenburg, estimated 60 MPH winds with tree limbs down in Cozad and a measured 56 MPH gust at the Lexington airport. However, this storm complex soon weakened below severe thresholds as it tracked deeper into the local area, and although isolated to scattered storms persisted across various parts of South Central Nebraska until around 5 a.m. CDT on the 29th there were no additional severe weather reports. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of the area averaged well-under 1 inch, with some pockets of 1-2 concentrated mainly within Nuckolls/Thayer counties from round 1.||Given that this event consisted of different rounds of storms separated in both location and time, it should come as no surprise that the upper air and surface situation was somewhat complex. Starting with the mid-upper levels, forcing was respectable for mid-August as South Central Nebraska was positioned downstream from a broad, slow-moving trough edging from the Northern Rockies toward the Dakotas. Embedded within this mean trough were several smaller-scale shortwaves, each helping to drive the separate rounds of convection. At the surface, a very slow-moving/weak cold front gradually worked its way southeastward through South Central Nebraska during the evening/overnight. Digging a bit deeper, round 1 during the early evening was largely driven by weak convergence and considerable instability focused along a pre-frontal surface trough stretched from southwest KS into southeast NE. Despite fairly weak deep-layer wind shear only around 30 knots, these storms quickly became severe in an environment featuring at least 2000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and surface dewpoints well into the low-mid 70s F. The second round of storms that arrived later in the night initially developed during the evening well to the west over the High Plains and was tied more closely to the primary mid level vort max approaching from WY and also the actual surface cold front. Even at this late hour, round 2 still had up to around 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE to work with.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were six to eight inch diameter tree limbs blown down from estimated 60 mph thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In only a few hours' time between the late evening of Sunday the 28th and the early morning of Monday the 29th, a nearly stationary cluster of thunderstorms drenched much of southern Adams, northern Webster and adjacent small portions of Franklin and Nuckolls counties with 3-7 inches of heavy rain, at least temporarily halting a worsening drought (more on this below). Extensive flooding ensued along the Little Blue River and its tributaries (including Ash Creek) and numerous rural county roads/bridges were flooded out/damaged, some of which remained barricaded for several days. The overall-worst flooding concentrated within south central Adams County, including the Crystal Lake Recreation Area and campground located just north of Ayr along the Little Blue River. There, extensive damage occurred to electrical boxes, buildings and picnic tables, and the park remained closed to the public for over two weeks before reopening in mid-September. Along the Little Blue itself, flood waters quickly worked downstream from Ayr, reaching the Pauline area on the morning of the 29th and the automated river gauge near Deweese by early evening. At this gauge site, the river crested around 11 feet (one foot above minor flood stage) during the overnight hours before falling below flood stage on the morning of the 30th. Minor flooding likely continued along the river through much of northeastern Nuckolls County, but no flooding was reported farther downstream into Thayer County. This event was all about flooding, as thunderstorms otherwise remained sub-severe and yielded no reports of large hail or damaging winds. ||This was likely the most notable flooding event to strike the Adams/Webster County line area in several decades, especially considering that the majority of accumulation occurred in only a four-hour period between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. CDT. Some of the most impressive rain totals featured: 7.27 two miles northeast of Blue Hill (NeRAIN observer), 6.62 four miles southwest of Blue Hill (NWS observer), 5.60 four miles south-southeast of Roseland (NeRAIN), 5.50 in Blue Hill (personal weather station) and 3.81 five miles south-southeast of Deweese (NeRAIN). In an ironic twist, leading up to this event the southern Adams/northern Webster locale had been one of the driest places in the entire state of Nebraska for the summer, having recently been placed in a Severe Drought (D2) category by the U.S. Drought Monitor. As a striking example of this sudden transition from drought to flood, the NWS cooperative observer near Blue Hill tallied merely 2.77 of rain from Jun 1-Aug 27 (roughly 25% of normal) and then received more than twice this amount (6.62) on this single night! ||Taking a closer look at event timing and evolution, things got underway between 9:30-10:30 p.m. CDT on the 28th as thunderstorms erupted along a northwest-southeast axis extending from western Adams County down into Republic/Washington counties in Kansas. Over the next several hours, the northwestern edge of this initial activity evolved into a quasi-stationary storm cluster that essentially anchored itself over the southern Adams/northern Webster area. By 2 a.m. CDT on the 29th, convection finally began to diminish in intensity/rainfall rates, leaving only lingering lighter showers that dissipated completely by 5 a.m. CDT. This was a rather sneaky mesoscale flood event, as although low chances for thunderstorms were in the forecast, computer models (even in the very short term) were not spotlighting the potential for significant flooding. Not surprisingly, forcing aloft was rather nebulous, not uncommon by late-August standards. In the mid-upper levels, the Central Plains resided under fairly weak south-southwest flow, directed between broad ridging over the southeastern CONUS and a slow-moving trough to the west anchored by a modest closed low over New Mexico. Parameters were not conducive to severe storms given deep-layer wind shear averaging well under 30 knots and weak mid-level lapse rates. However, favorable factors for slow-moving, heavy rainers included: the presence of subtle mid-level waves, precipitable water values averaging over 1.5 and slightly-enhanced flow between 850-700 millibars during the night, resulting in increased deep moisture convergence. At the surface, there were no notable features to speak of, although dewpoints averaging in the mid-60s F were indicative of the moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3-7 inches of rain fell across mainly the southern third of Adams County. Extensive flooding ensued along the Little Blue River and its tributaries (including Ash Creek) and numerous rural county roads/bridges were flooded out/damaged, some of which remained barricaded for several days. The overall-worst flooding concentrated within south central parts of the county, including the Crystal Lake Recreation Area and campground located just north of Ayr along the Little Blue River. The Clay County Emergency Manager happened to be camping there that night and reported that the campground was flooding and that nearby Highway 281 was down to one lane in places. At the campground, extensive damage occurred to electrical boxes, buildings and picnic tables, and the park remained closed to the public for over two weeks before reopening in mid-September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced mostly hail along with a little wind damage, and excessive rainfall on this Tuesday afternoon and evening. The first thunderstorms began forming just after 3 pm and were part of a broken line of isolated storms that extended from O'neill south to near Kearney, and then southwest into Kansas. A dominant storm developed along the Hall-Howard County line by 4 p.m.. This storm became severe as it continued east, affecting adjoining portions of Hamilton and Merrick Counties through 5:30 pm CST. Around 4:50 pm CST, this storm produced a gustnado near Chapman, along with hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter, and estimated winds around 70 mph. After 5:30 pm, other storms developed in close-proximity, displaying multicell characteristics. An east-west oriented line segment of storms formed across Hamilton and York Counties. This line remained intact until 8 p.m. CST. Storms within this line trained, producing severe hail up to the size of golf balls and flash flooding from several inches of rain. Outflow winds overturned an irrigation pivot and produced blowing dust that reduced the visibility to near zero near Bradshaw. Several reports of gustnadoes were received along the leading edge of the outflow. ||Meanwhile, one storm crossed into Furnas County from Kansas beginning around 4 p.m. CST. Several other thunderstorms developed around it, forming a small mostly sub-severe cluster. The initial storm briefly became severe, producing estimated winds of 85 mph in northern Furnas County. This cluster moved northeast and eventually formed a short line segment that was oriented from northwest to southeast. This line produced winds of 50 to 55 mph in a couple locations in Adams and Clay Counties. It then crossed Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties from 7 to 9 p.m. CST, adding to the already impressive rainfall amounts. Much of northeastern Hamilton County received 4 to 6 inches of rain in less than 6 hours. The highest total was 6.77 inches at Marquette. These high rainfall amounts were surrounded by a larger area of 2 to 3 inches which extended across Hall, Hamilton, and York Counties. This rain resulted in extensive flooding over northeastern Hamilton County and along the Big Blue River in Polk County.||Occluding low pressure was along the Montana-North Dakota border on this day. The triple point was over South Dakota, with a quasi-stationary warm front extending east through the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic States. The cold front was advancing eastward across Nebraska, and was moving through south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours. In the upper levels, a broad trough covered much of the Unites States, anchored by an intensifying upper-low over Montana. A closed high was over Ontario, Canada. Temperatures in the pre-storm environment climbed to around 80 degrees, with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor resulting in MLCAPE of not more than 1500 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 30 to 35 knots.

This rain resulted in extensive flooding over northeastern Hamilton County and along the Big Blue River in Polk County.||Occluding low pressure was along the Montana-North Dakota border on this day. The triple point was over South Dakota, with a quasi-stationary warm front extending east through the Midwest and Mid-Atlantic States. The cold front was advancing eastward across Nebraska, and was moving through south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours. In the upper levels, a broad trough covered much of the Unites States, anchored by an intensifying upper-low over Montana. A closed high was over Ontario, Canada. Temperatures in the pre-storm environment climbed to around 80 degrees, with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor resulting in MLCAPE of not more than 1500 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 30 to 35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall report was 6.77 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced mostly hail along with a little wind damage, and excessive rainfall on this Tuesday afternoon and evening. The first thunderstorms began forming just after 3 pm and were part of a broken line of isolated storms that extended from O'neill south to near Kearney, and then southwest into Kansas. A dominant storm developed along the Hall-Howard County line by 4 p.m.. This storm became severe as it continued east, affecting adjoining portions of Hamilton and Merrick Counties through 5:30 pm CST. Around 4:50 pm CST, this storm produced a gustnado near Chapman, along with hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter, and estimated winds around 70 mph. After 5:30 pm, other storms developed in close-proximity, displaying multicell characteristics. An east-west oriented line segment of storms formed across Hamilton and York Counties. This line remained intact until 8 p.m. CST. Storms within this line trained, producing severe hail up to the size of golf balls and flash flooding from several inches of rain. Outflow winds overturned an irrigation pivot and produced blowing dust that reduced the visibility to near zero near Bradshaw. Several reports of gustnadoes were received along the leading edge of the outflow. ||Meanwhile, one storm crossed into Furnas County from Kansas beginning around 4 p.m. CST. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms produced severe hail on this Monday late in the afternoon and into the early evening. Between noon and 1 p.m. CST, scattered showers developed over Webster and Adams Counties. There were a few embedded rumbles of thunder. These showers expanded and lifted northeast into Hall, Hamilton, and Merrick Counties. A brief non-supercell tornado touched down near Juniata, in Adams County, around 1:40 p.m. CST. This tornado occurred in the absence of any lightning and rain, although there was a shower off to the east. By 4 p.m. CST, a couple of the showers strengthened into thunderstorms. Scattered thunderstorms actually started increasing in coverage after 6 p.m. CST as an approaching shortwave trough began influencing the region. Several storms produced at least quarter size hail over Clay, Hall, Hamilton, and Webster Counties. Larger hail did occur, with tennis ball size being the largest, reported near Giltner, in southwestern Hamilton County. Car windows were broken. Between 6:30 p.m. and 8:30 p.m. CST, storms formed into a southwest-northeast oriented line across Hall and Hamilton Counties, resulting in cell-training. This resulted in rainfall totals exceeding 2 inches over Grand Island, including 2.72 inches on the northeast side of the city. Some urban flash flooding occurred, with roads being closed due to flooded underpasses. Manhole covers were also removed in some locations. The thunderstorm activity had moved east and south of south central Nebraska by 11 p.m. CST.||A north-northeast to south-southwest oriented quasi-stationary front extended from the eastern Dakota's through western Kansas into New Mexico. A lee-side low was near Colorado Springs. In the upper levels broad southwest flow was over much of the United States, with a ridge over the Great Lakes, and an elongated trough from Hudson Bay to central California. A weak shortwave trough was passing over the Central Plains. When the first storms initiated, late in the afternoon, temperatures were in the middle 70s, with dewpoints in the lower 60s. MLCAPE was between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 40 to 45 knots. This environment was more than supportive of organized, persistent storms.

Thunderstorms developed in two areas on the evening August 16th. The first area was over northeast Nebraska along a weak stalled boundary, and the second area was along an outflow boundary over southeast Nebraska. The combination of favorable instability and shear in both of these areas allowed for marginal supercell structures and severe weather, mainly in the form of large hail. The thunderstorms dissipated by late evening with the loss of surface heating.

A cold front slowly moved south during the afternoon on August 4th into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. The front neared the Interstate 80 corridor by late afternoon. In addition, an outflow boundary from early storms in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa was lifting north. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during peak heating along these two boundaries. Due to the combination of the thunderstorms developing on the stalling boundaries and very steep low-level lapse rates two landspout tornadoes were observed. Otherwise the thunderstorms were largely non-severe with gusty non-severe winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado was reported via social media. The tornado was small and last only a couple of minutes with no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into northeast Nebraska during the day on August 23rd, warm and moist air returned northward into the area along a warm front. As temperatures increased into the mid and upper 80s along and south of the warm front, thunderstorms began to develop by late afternoon. These thunderstorms continued to increase in coverage and intensity during the evening. Favorable shear along the warm front allowed for some of the storms to form into supercells and produce severe weather. Late in the evening the thunderstorms formed into a complex and moved southeast across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa with severe weather and heavy rainfall occurring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In only a few hours' time between the late evening of Sunday the 28th and the early morning of Monday the 29th, a nearly stationary cluster of thunderstorms drenched much of southern Adams, northern Webster and adjacent small portions of Franklin and Nuckolls counties with 3-7 inches of heavy rain, at least temporarily halting a worsening drought (more on this below). Extensive flooding ensued along the Little Blue River and its tributaries (including Ash Creek) and numerous rural county roads/bridges were flooded out/damaged, some of which remained barricaded for several days. The overall-worst flooding concentrated within south central Adams County, including the Crystal Lake Recreation Area and campground located just north of Ayr along the Little Blue River. There, extensive damage occurred to electrical boxes, buildings and picnic tables, and the park remained closed to the public for over two weeks before reopening in mid-September. Along the Little Blue itself, flood waters quickly worked downstream from Ayr, reaching the Pauline area on the morning of the 29th and the automated river gauge near Deweese by early evening. At this gauge site, the river crested around 11 feet (one foot above minor flood stage) during the overnight hours before falling below flood stage on the morning of the 30th. Minor flooding likely continued along the river through much of northeastern Nuckolls County, but no flooding was reported farther downstream into Thayer County. This event was all about flooding, as thunderstorms otherwise remained sub-severe and yielded no reports of large hail or damaging winds. ||This was likely the most notable flooding event to strike the Adams/Webster County line area in several decades, especially considering that the majority of accumulation occurred in only a four-hour period between 10 p.m. and 2 a.m. CDT. Some of the most impressive rain totals featured: 7.27 two miles northeast of Blue Hill (NeRAIN observer), 6.62 four miles southwest of Blue Hill (NWS observer), 5.60 four miles south-southeast of Roseland (NeRAIN), 5.50 in Blue Hill (personal weather station) and 3.81 five miles south-southeast of Deweese (NeRAIN). In an ironic twist, leading up to this event the southern Adams/northern Webster locale had been one of the driest places in the entire state of Nebraska for the summer, having recently been placed in a Severe Drought (D2) category by the U.S. Drought Monitor. As a striking example of this sudden transition from drought to flood, the NWS cooperative observer near Blue Hill tallied merely 2.77 of rain from Jun 1-Aug 27 (roughly 25% of normal) and then received more than twice this amount (6.62) on this single night! ||Taking a closer look at event timing and evolution, things got underway between 9:30-10:30 p.m. CDT on the 28th as thunderstorms erupted along a northwest-southeast axis extending from western Adams County down into Republic/Washington counties in Kansas. Over the next several hours, the northwestern edge of this initial activity evolved into a quasi-stationary storm cluster that essentially anchored itself over the southern Adams/northern Webster area. By 2 a.m. CDT on the 29th, convection finally began to diminish in intensity/rainfall rates, leaving only lingering lighter showers that dissipated completely by 5 a.m. CDT. This was a rather sneaky mesoscale flood event, as although low chances for thunderstorms were in the forecast, computer models (even in the very short term) were not spotlighting the potential for significant flooding. Not surprisingly, forcing aloft was rather nebulous, not uncommon by late-August standards. In the mid-upper levels, the Central Plains resided under fairly weak south-southwest flow, directed between broad ridging over the southeastern CONUS and a slow-moving trough to the west anchored by a modest closed low over New Mexico. Parameters were not conducive to severe storms given deep-layer wind shear averaging well under 30 knots and weak mid-level lapse rates. However, favorable factors for slow-moving, heavy rainers included: the presence of subtle mid-level waves, precipitable water values averaging over 1.5 and slightly-enhanced flow between 850-700 millibars during the night, resulting in increased deep moisture convergence. At the surface, there were no notable features to speak of, although dewpoints averaging in the mid-60s F were indicative of the moist airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3-6 inches of rain fell across mainly the northern third of Webster County, resulting in extensive flooding. The rain began around 930 pm CDT on August 28th and ended at about 430 am CDT on August 29th, although the bulk of the heaviest rain occurred between 10 pm CDT on August 28th to 2 am CDT on August 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level storm system moved into the northern Plains, a warm front retreated through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the afternoon. Unseasonably warm and humid air lifted into the region creating marginal instability by mid afternoon, but with the warm front in the area environmental shear was strong. Thunderstorms developed across northeast and east-central Nebraska by mid afternoon. Initially isolated supercells were the dominate mode with large hail the main threat. A small short-lived tornado did develop from one of the supercells in Washington County. Thunderstorms then moved into western Iowa as they continued to produce large hail. By mid evening the thunderstorms developed a common outflow in a west to east orientation and allowed for training, especially over northeast Nebraska. This continued regeneration of thunderstorms over the same area led to significant river and flash flooding over parts of the through the night.
Thunderstorms produced large hail in Scottsbluff and Box Butte counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball size hail was reported three miles north of Scottsbluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail in Scottsbluff and Box Butte counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported two miles west of Alliance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced strong winds over eastern Dawes County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at the Chadron Airport measured a peak gust of 66 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of downbursts occurred across northwest and northeast Omaha. The majority of the damage was centered Sorensen Parkway where significant tree, power line and pole damage was observed. Large tree limbs were blown down and some trees were uprooted. Some damage did occur at Eppley Airfield where small planes were moved or flipped. This closed the airport for some time after the storms passed. A maximum wind gust of 96 mph was recorded at the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated thunderstorms developed along a southwest to northeast oriented wind shift line across southwest and north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds blew over a large evergreen tree near the town bank and knocked over the powerline.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at the Chadron Airport measured a peak gust of 73 mph.

Two tornadoes occurred along with hail up to the size of golf balls on this Monday afternoon. A few very isolated general thunderstorms developed across south central Nebraska beginning around 1:30 p.m. CST. However, storms did not become severe until after 3 p.m. CST. Severe hail up to the size of golf balls occurred with some storms, which was impressive given the weak shear and pulse-type nature of the storms. A non-supercell tornado occurred near Odessa, in Buffalo County. Somehow, a lone supercell managed to develop over Clay County, and it was responsible for producing a tornado and hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter. Both of these tornadoes were rated EF-0. By 7 p.m. CST, all of the thunderstorm activity had dissipated or moved out of south central Nebraska.||Surface low pressure was quasi-stationary over western Kansas. The warm front extending east from the low lifted into central Nebraska and became stationary. Just prior to most of the thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the lower to middle 70s with dewpoints in the middle to upper 50s. With mid-level lapse rates near 8 degrees C/km, this resulted in MLCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg. Effective deep layer shear was 15-20 knots, barely supportive of multicell storm mode. In the upper levels, a longwave trough was over the western U.S. with a lead low exiting Nebraska into South Dakota, and a new more potent low dropping into the backside of the trough over the Northern Rockies.

As a cold front moved into northeast Nebraska during the day on August 23rd, warm and moist air returned northward into the area along a warm front. As temperatures increased into the mid and upper 80s along and south of the warm front, thunderstorms began to develop by late afternoon. These thunderstorms continued to increase in coverage and intensity during the evening. Favorable shear along the warm front allowed for some of the storms to form into supercells and produce severe weather. Late in the evening the thunderstorms formed into a complex and moved southeast across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa with severe weather and heavy rainfall occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rapidly rotating wall cloud was observed to the north northeast of the town of Washington. The wall cloud produced a funnel that briefly had a condensation to the surface with a dust debris cloud present. No damage was observed in the area from the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms develop late in the evening of July 17 in response to the nocturnal strengthening of the low-level jet over a stalled frontal boundary over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Although the majority of these thunderstorms were strong, only isolated reports of severe weather were observed mainly in the form of strong winds. In addition to the severe weather, the orientation of the thunderstorms from northwest to southeast resulted in repeated or training of the storms and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated winds to near 60 mph. There was also 6 to 8 inch tree branches knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate rounds of strong to isolated severe thunderstorms raked across eastern portions of South Central Nebraska on this Tuesday, the first mainly during the pre-dawn hours and the second during the mid-late afternoon. These storms resulted in a handful of large hail and heavy rain reports, highlighted by stones up to 2��� diameter in Bruning during the afternoon and an unofficial calendar-day rainfall total of 4.70��� in Milligan. ||Starting with the initial early morning round, scattered to numerous elevated storms blossomed within counties east of the Highway 281 corridor between 1-3 a.m. CDT, forming in rather classic nocturnal fashion along the axis of a stout 50 knot southerly low level jet evident at 850 millibars. Convection continuously redeveloped and streamed along a north-south oriented corridor for several more hours, even past sunrise, before the final morning storms finally vacated South Central Nebraska between 9:30-10:30 a.m. CDT.  While most morning storms produced only small hail and locally heavy rain up to around 2���, the presence of modest most-unstable CAPE of at least 500 J/kg resulted in a few strong to marginally severe cells, including one that dumped hail up to ping pong ball size on Aurora around 3:30 a.m. CDT. ||The vast majority of South Central Nebraska had a storm-free lull between late morning and mid-afternoon before the next round of surface-based convection initiated between 3-4 p.m. CDT over Nuckolls, Clay and Hamilton counties along a slow-moving cold front. During the ensuing three hours from 4-7 p.m. CDT, a north-south axis of mainly multicell and quasi-linear storms edged slowly east, primarily affecting the Highway 81 counties before exiting into eastern Nebraska around sunset. The most intense storm of the afternoon tracked through northern Thayer and southern Fillmore counties between 4:30-6:00 p.m. CDT, yielding several reports of quarter to golf ball size hail in the Carleton and Bruning areas, including a few stones up to 2��� in Bruning. Rainfall-wise, these storms dumped generally 1-2��� over some of the same eastern real estate that already saw similar amounts during the morning. As a result, localized pockets of the area tallied 3+��� for the calendar day, although there were no reports of significant flooding. A few of the highest reported daily rain totals featured: 4.70��� in Milligan, 4.05��� in Polk, 3.44��� near Carleton and 3.08��� in Bradshaw. ||Taking a closer look at the meteorological setup for the afternoon round, this was a fairly strongly-forced event in the mid-upper levels, as a potent shortwave trough swung out of CO into the heart of the Central Plains. At the surface, a rather well-defined and slow-moving, north-south oriented cold front provided a focus for storm initiation in eastern counties. Despite surface dewpoints along/ahead of the front rising into the low-mid 60s F, considerable daytime cloud cover helped hold down mixed-layer CAPE over the local area, with generally no more than 1000 J/kg. However, both deep-layer wind shear (around 50 knots) and low-level wind shear (around 30 knots) were rather strong, compensating somewhat for the lack of instability. On the regional scale, South Central Nebraska narrowly missed out on a more widespread severe storm event that impacted parts of central and eastern KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of half dollars and torrential rainfall was reported.

Thunderstorms developed over areas of western South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of July 6th. These thunderstorms moved east and developed upscale into a large convective complex that moved into northeast Nebraska during the late evening on the 6th of July. The line of thunderstorms moved southeast across all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa with the primary severe weather threat being from damaging winds. The thunderstorms exited into northeast Kansas and northern Missouri during the early morning. This complex of thunderstorms then left an outflow boundary across the area, which additional thunderstorms developed along during the afternoon of July 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate rounds of strong to isolated severe thunderstorms raked across eastern portions of South Central Nebraska on this Tuesday, the first mainly during the pre-dawn hours and the second during the mid-late afternoon. These storms resulted in a handful of large hail and heavy rain reports, highlighted by stones up to 2��� diameter in Bruning during the afternoon and an unofficial calendar-day rainfall total of 4.70��� in Milligan. ||Starting with the initial early morning round, scattered to numerous elevated storms blossomed within counties east of the Highway 281 corridor between 1-3 a.m. CDT, forming in rather classic nocturnal fashion along the axis of a stout 50 knot southerly low level jet evident at 850 millibars. Convection continuously redeveloped and streamed along a north-south oriented corridor for several more hours, even past sunrise, before the final morning storms finally vacated South Central Nebraska between 9:30-10:30 a.m. CDT.  While most morning storms produced only small hail and locally heavy rain up to around 2���, the presence of modest most-unstable CAPE of at least 500 J/kg resulted in a few strong to marginally severe cells, including one that dumped hail up to ping pong ball size on Aurora around 3:30 a.m. CDT. ||The vast majority of South Central Nebraska had a storm-free lull between late morning and mid-afternoon before the next round of surface-based convection initiated between 3-4 p.m. CDT over Nuckolls, Clay and Hamilton counties along a slow-moving cold front. During the ensuing three hours from 4-7 p.m. CDT, a north-south axis of mainly multicell and quasi-linear storms edged slowly east, primarily affecting the Highway 81 counties before exiting into eastern Nebraska around sunset. The most intense storm of the afternoon tracked through northern Thayer and southern Fillmore counties between 4:30-6:00 p.m. CDT, yielding several reports of quarter to golf ball size hail in the Carleton and Bruning areas, including a few stones up to 2��� in Bruning. Rainfall-wise, these storms dumped generally 1-2��� over some of the same eastern real estate that already saw similar amounts during the morning. As a result, localized pockets of the area tallied 3+��� for the calendar day, although there were no reports of significant flooding. A few of the highest reported daily rain totals featured: 4.70��� in Milligan, 4.05��� in Polk, 3.44��� near Carleton and 3.08��� in Bradshaw. ||Taking a closer look at the meteorological setup for the afternoon round, this was a fairly strongly-forced event in the mid-upper levels, as a potent shortwave trough swung out of CO into the heart of the Central Plains. At the surface, a rather well-defined and slow-moving, north-south oriented cold front provided a focus for storm initiation in eastern counties. Despite surface dewpoints along/ahead of the front rising into the low-mid 60s F, considerable daytime cloud cover helped hold down mixed-layer CAPE over the local area, with generally no more than 1000 J/kg. However, both deep-layer wind shear (around 50 knots) and low-level wind shear (around 30 knots) were rather strong, compensating somewhat for the lack of instability. On the regional scale, South Central Nebraska narrowly missed out on a more widespread severe storm event that impacted parts of central and eastern KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A daily rainfall total of 3.05 inches was reported from separate rounds of morning and afternoon thunderstorms.

A very hot and humid airmass resided over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the evening of July 5th. This led to extreme instability over the area. A cluster of thunderstorms developed over central Nebraska and moved east northeast into this atmosphere. These thunderstorms were response for wind damage, some significant, as they moved across eastern Nebraska and into west central Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and very humid weather, with daytime temperatures reaching the 95 to 100 degree range, brought the heat index to 100 to 115 degrees during the afternoon hours of July 20th through July 23rd. Emergency rooms at hospitals reported that an unknown number of people suffered heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, although no lasting heat related illnesses were reported. Dew points in the 70s over the area reflected the very high humidity, with dew points at a few places peaking at or just above 80 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures reaching daytime highs of 95 to 100 were accompanied by very humid conditions, with the heat index rising to 100 to 115 degrees. An unknown number of people suffered from heat stress, heat exhaustion, or dehydration, as reported by hospital emergency rooms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from nickels to ping pong balls was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over areas of western South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of July 6th. These thunderstorms moved east and developed upscale into a large convective complex that moved into northeast Nebraska during the late evening on the 6th of July. The line of thunderstorms moved southeast across all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa with the primary severe weather threat being from damaging winds. The thunderstorms exited into northeast Kansas and northern Missouri during the early morning. This complex of thunderstorms then left an outflow boundary across the area, which additional thunderstorms developed along during the afternoon of July 7th.
